text:
The U.S. is the birthplace of BMX Racing, with the sport starting in the 70s in southern California on dirt tracks - but it has yielded just one Olympic champion in 16 years, something Team GB hero and 2008 Team Sprint gold medalist Jamie Staff MBE is hoping to help change in Paris this summer. The sport exploded in the U.S. in the 70s and in Europe and the U.K. in the 80s, when owning a BMX bike was a must-have for children and teenagers.

It took 38 years for BMX racers to take to the skies of theOlympic Games, and since its introduction in Beijing in 2008, it's been a must-watch event. Eight of the best riders, men and women, will line up at the gate and wait for the lights to go out before racing down an 8m-high, 35-degree start hill while reaching speeds as high as 60km/h, putting their bodies on the line to fight for the right to be crowned Olympic champion. Connor Fields is the only American to date to claim an Olympic title in the discipline after crossing the line first in Rio in 2016, but with a stacked roster, Team USA is cautiously optimistic heading into Friday's semis and finals on the Saint Quentin-en-Yvelines track.

UCI World Champion Alise Willoughby appears to be Team USA's best shot - but she faces a tough fight against on-fire Aussie Saya Sakakibara, and reigning Olympic champion Bethany Shriever of Team GB. Willoughby will look to stamp her authority down against Sakakibara in the first semi-final while teammate Daleny Vaughn lines up against Shriever in the second. Double Olympic champion of Colombia, Mariana Pajón, has also progressed to Friday's racing. Eight will progress to the women's final scheduled for 8:50 p.m. GMT.

On the men's side, Cameron Wood and Kamren Larsen will be representing the red, white, and blue. A new Olympic champion will be crowned too, with Dutchman Niek Kimmann missing the Paris Games due to an inflamed heart muscle. Tokyo silver medalist Kye Whyte has looked off form as well, while the French at times have looked unbeatable, with the men's final set for 8:35 p.m. GMT.



"Alise Willoughby [the] world champion. She's kind of on point right now. It was hard [Thursday] - we didn't have any splits on the track so it's kind of hard to see how she compares to some of the others besides the lap times. But we're kind of banking on Elise getting a medal," USA Cycling High-Performance Director Staff MBE, a BMX Racing legend and 2008 Team GB Olympic gold medalist in the Track Cycling Team Sprint alongside Sir Chris Hoy, Sir Jason Kenny, toldExpress Sport.

"I know she's more than capable of it. So, It's just a matter of her performing under pressure - But we expect semi-finals to go pretty straightforward, to be honest, and then Daleny Vaughn, she's actually riding really well. Mentally, I think she's coping with the pressure of the event really well as well. She's got a good support network around her coaches out here as well as all my coaching team around her and I think her entire family - I'm not kidding - is out here as well.

"I think she feels well supported. I think that's helping her case as well she has an extremely strong start. She just needs to kind of perfect the first jump a little bit and some of the other jumps. But I think she'll definitely stick her nose in that first turn. Watch for her. It'd be amazing if she could make the final."



The U.S. has a monster starter with Larsen well revered for his ability to snap out of the gate quickly. "He's kind of known now on the circuit of being the quickest starter," Staff said on Larsen, 24. "I think if he just keeps up his what he was doing yesterday he has every chance of making the final."

Wood, 22, also has impressive qualities, which Staff made sure to highlight. "Cam [Wood] is not as quick at the start but really good acceleration down the ramp and quicker around the track than Larsen," Staff said. "As long as Cam Wood doesn't get shut off, I think he's absolutely got a chance of making the final. Our goal is to make the final for our four remaining athletes - but we know it's BMX and things happen. There's so many variables that these riders face."

The U.S. has five Olympic medals in 16 years across the men's and women's and just one gold from Fields eight years ago. "One medal," Staff said on the team's goal for Friday. "Three is kind of exceptional, right? Three is all the stars aligned and you had an amazing day. So I think we can get anywhere between one and three. [We would] Obviously be disappointed if we don't get any."

Don't miss:Novak Djokovic shows true colours with message to Andy Murray after retirement



The threats to the U.S. are clear: Sakakibara and the defending champion Shriever in the women's, and the French trio of UCI World Champion Joris Daudet, Sylvain André, and Romain Mahieu in the men. Team GB silver medalist Whyte appears to be nursing a back injury but don't put it past the Prince of Peckham to pull a result out of the bag, though.

"I think other nations obviously the French have an extremely strong team in the men's," Staff continued. "And they're going to be absolutely hard to beat.

"Beyond that, Kye Whyte doesn't seem to be on point, I know he's dealing with a back issue. Maybe he's not as strong as he could be. The French are going to be the ones with targets on the back and hard to beat, and as I said, it's BMX racing and anything can happen."

title: Meet Team GB 2008 Olympic hero, now U.S. BMX Racing director at Paris Games | Other | Sport | Express.co.uk
author: Joshua Mbu
description: EXCLUSIVE: BMX Racing legend and 2008 Team GB Olympic gold medalist Jamie Staff is directing the U.S roster at the Paris Games.
